title: Guide Ribbons
desc: The route ribbons through the crystal lanes have frayed again, which is how visitors end up bleeding on expensive geology.

steps.text:
Crystal Cave punishes uncertainty faster than almost any OverWorld work site. Hesitate in the wrong lane and you catch a sleeve on a shard. Turn too wide and you scrape skin on something older and harder than most tools. That is why the safer routes are marked with silk guide ribbons, floor pegs, and narrow visual lines that help creatures move through the cave without decorating it with blood.

The route keeper holds up a frayed strip of red-marked silk and gives you a look that implies the ribbon should be embarrassed. "Replace the upper guides if you like climbing," she says. "Reset the floor pegs if you prefer crouching around sharp things. The lanes need both. Today you only have time for one."

options.label: Replace the upper silk ribbons through the narrow lanes
Reset the floor pegs and lane markers below

steps.text:
The upper ribbons run from crystal spur to crystal spur, tied high enough to read at a glance and light enough not to pull against delicate formations. You cut away the frayed sections, thread fresh silk through the anchor eyes, and draw each line taut so the route bends are obvious long before a traveler reaches them. It is careful work because every anchor point sits beside something sharp, polished, and entirely uninterested in your knuckles.

Once the new guides are in place, the lane looks readable again instead of speculative. The ribbon line catches the cave light and carries it forward in little flashes, leading the eye around each dangerous turn. The route keeper walks the path once, never breaking stride, and seems satisfied. "Good. Now the cave can go back to hurting only the careless."

options.label: Coil the old silk and leave the upper lane legible

steps.text:
The floor pegs are simple markers, bronze pins set low into the stone wherever the safest line would otherwise be easy to miss. In Crystal Cave, simple markers earn a great deal of gratitude. Three pegs have loosened, one is half buried in fallen grit, and another has shifted just enough to send a boot toward a crystal needle instead of away from it.

You reseat each peg, clear the surrounding grit, and sight the line from the lane entrance until the route reads cleanly again from one marker to the next. It is less graceful work than the ribbons above, but more honest. By the time you finish, the lower lane no longer asks anyone to improvise. The route keeper checks the alignment with a short nod. "There. A proper path. The crystals may keep their edges. We will keep our ankles."

options.label: Dust off the pegs and reopen the lower route
